response:
```json
{
  "diagnosis": "This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to extremely small access granularity. With 819,200 total I/O operations (409,600 reads + 409,600 writes) transferring only 26.2MB total data, the average transfer size is just 64 bytes per operation. This creates massive syscall overhead - the job spends 18.88% of runtime on metadata operations alone. The 819,144 unaligned accesses further compound the problem. At 28.1 MB/s bandwidth, this job is severely underperforming due to the 1:1 ratio of small I/O operations.",

  "recommendations": [
    {
      "priority": 1,
      "bottleneck_dimension": "access_granularity", 
      "explanation": "The job performs 819,200 I/O operations with 64-byte transfers each, causing excessive syscall overhead. Each small write/read requires a separate system call, creating ~819K syscalls instead of ~26 large ones.",
      "code_before": "for (i=0; i<409600; i++) {\n    write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */\n    read(fd, buf, 64);   /* 64B per call */\n}",
      "code_after": "/* Buffer small writes, flush in 1MB chunks */\nchar big_buf[1048576];\nsize_t offset = 0;\nfor (i=0; i<409600; i++) {\n    memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64);\n    offset += 64;\n    if (offset >= 1048576) {\n        write(fd, big_buf, offset);\n        offset = 0;\n    }\n}\nif (offset > 0) write(fd, big_buf, offset);  /* flush remainder */",
      "expected_speedup": "15-25x based on reducing 819K syscalls to ~26 large transfers",
      "kb_citation": "ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3_2, ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1_0, ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2_1",
      "confidence": "high",
      "api_change": "POSIX write buffering strategy - same API, different usage pattern"
    }
  ],
  "overall_expected_improvement": "15-25x speedup (from 28.1 MB/s to 420-700 MB/s) by eliminating syscall overhead through I/O aggregation"
}
```
system_prompt:
You are an HPC I/O performance expert. You analyze Darshan profiling data
and provide specific, actionable code-level optimization recommendations.

RULES:
1. Every recommendation MUST be grounded in the benchmark evidence provided.
2. Include specific before/after code snippets showing the exact change.
3. Cite the KB entry ID for each recommendation.
4. Quantify expected improvement using benchmark measurements only.
5. Do NOT fabricate performance numbers or API calls that don't exist.
6. Prioritize recommendations by expected impact (highest speedup first).
7. Use standard HPC I/O APIs: POSIX (read/write), MPI-IO (MPI_File_*), HDF5 (H5D*).
user_prompt:
Analyze this HPC job's I/O behavior and provide code-level optimization recommendations.

## Detected Bottlenecks (ML classifier, Micro-F1=0.923):
  - **access_granularity** (confidence=1.00): I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive syscall overhead

## Key Contributing Features (SHAP per-label attribution):

  access_granularity:
    - POSIX_FILE_NOT_ALIGNED = 819144.0000 (|SHAP|=3.4106, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_ACCESS1_COUNT = 819200.0000 (|SHAP|=1.7999, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_WRITES = 409600.0000 (|SHAP|=1.4248, increases_risk)
    - has_apmpi = 0.0000 (|SHAP|=1.0529, decreases_risk)
    - POSIX_READS = 409600.0000 (|SHAP|=0.8455, increases_risk)


## Benchmark Evidence (verified Knowledge Base with source code):

  --- KB Entry 1 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3_2) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r3
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 2 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1_0) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r1
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 3 (ID: ior_ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2_1) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_small_posix_t64_n4_r2
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)  /* src/aiori-POSIX.c:250 */
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* src/aiori-MPIIO.c:200 */
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }


## Job Darshan Summary:
  nprocs: 4.0
  runtime_seconds: 0.9844
  POSIX_BYTES_WRITTEN: 26214400.0
  avg_write_size: 64.0
